notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Looking at your connected accounts, all five show $0 balances — your Capital One 360 Checking, Bluevine Business Checking, Chase Ink Business Preferred, American Express Blue Business Plus, and Ally Tax Reserve Savings were all added today (June 5) as manual accounts, so balances haven't been entered yet.
That said, I can work with what I do know about your financial picture to give you a meaningful answer.
What I can see
Your transaction history tells an important story. In May, you brought in $13,960 in freelance income (from Figma Design Systems, Notion UX Research, and a Stripe payment) and spent $5,503 — generating roughly $8,457 in net cash flow. You also made a $4,200 estimated tax payment in April. This paints a picture of a self-employed UX consultant with variable but healthy income, marketplace health insurance, no employer benefits, and a lean operating structure.
On crypto and concentration risk
Since your account balances are currently showing as $0, I can't confirm whether you actually hold any crypto or calculate its percentage of your total net worth. A few possibilities worth exploring:
No crypto connected: Origin doesn't currently support direct crypto trading or custody, so if you hold crypto on an exchange (Coinbase, Kraken, etc.), it wouldn't appear here unless you've manually added it as an asset.
Balances not yet entered: Since your accounts were just connected today as manual accounts, balances may simply not have been populated yet.
Concentration risk framework for your situation
Given that you're self-employed with uneven income (as I recall you've mentioned preferring stable, tax-safe financial systems), crypto concentration carries a compounding risk that's worth thinking through carefully:
Income volatility + asset volatility is a double exposure — if a slow client month coincides with a crypto drawdown, liquidity pressure hits from both sides simultaneously
No employer safety net means your emergency fund and liquid assets are doing more heavy lifting than for a salaried person
Tax complexity: Crypto is taxed as property, so every sale or exchange is a taxable event — this layers on top of your already-complex Schedule C situation
A general guideline many financial planners use: speculative or highly volatile assets like crypto should represent no more than 5-10% of investable assets, and for self-employed individuals with variable income, erring toward the lower end is typically more prudent.
To give you a precise concentration percentage, could you share approximately what your crypto holdings are worth and your rough total net worth (savings, investments, etc.)? That way I can give you a specific and grounded assessment rather than a general framework.
Note: Investment calculations and projections are for illustrative purposes only. Market conditions change frequently. Consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
